cards.title: The CPU and Von Neumann Architecture
lines: The CPU (Central Processing Unit) processes instructions and data to run programs.
In the Von Neumann model, instructions and data share the same memory.
The MAR (Memory Address Register) holds the address of the location to read or write.
The MDR (Memory Data Register) holds the data or instruction being transferred.
The Program Counter (PC) holds the address of the next instruction to fetch.
The Accumulator stores the result of calculations from the ALU.
The ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) performs calculations and logic operations.
The CU (Control Unit) directs the fetch-decode-execute cycle and coordinates components.

cards.title: CPU Performance Factors
lines: The CPU repeatedly fetches, decodes, and executes instructions stored in memory.
Clock speed is measured in hertz and sets how many cycles run each second.
A higher clock speed means more instructions can be processed per second.
A larger cache stores frequently used data close to the CPU to reduce delays.
More cores allow the CPU to process several instructions at the same time.
An embedded system is a computer built into a larger device to do one dedicated task.

cards.title: Units of Data
lines: A bit is a single binary digit, either 0 or 1.
A nibble is 4 bits and a byte is 8 bits.
1 kilobyte is 1000 bytes and 1 megabyte is 1000 kilobytes.
1 gigabyte is 1000 megabytes and 1 terabyte is 1000 gigabytes.
A kibibyte (KiB) is 1024 bytes, using powers of two rather than ten.
Denary is base 10, binary is base 2, and hexadecimal is base 16.

cards.title: Binary, Hexadecimal and Two's Complement
lines: Each binary place value doubles: 128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1.
Hexadecimal uses digits 0 to 9 and letters A to F, where A is 10 and F is 15.
One hexadecimal digit represents exactly four binary bits.
Overflow happens when a binary addition result is too large for the available bits.
A logical shift left multiplies a binary number by two for each place moved.
Two's complement represents negative numbers by flipping the bits and adding one.

cards.title: The Three Programming Constructs
lines: Sequence means instructions are carried out one after another in order.
Selection chooses between actions using IF, ELSE, and CASE statements.
Iteration repeats instructions using count-controlled or condition-controlled loops.
A count-controlled loop repeats a set number of times, such as a FOR loop.
A condition-controlled loop repeats while or until a condition is met.
Basic data types include integer, real, Boolean, character, and string.
Casting converts a value from one data type to another.

cards.title: RAM, ROM and Storage Types
lines: RAM is volatile and temporarily holds programs and data currently in use.
ROM is non-volatile and stores the start-up instructions needed to boot the computer.
Virtual memory uses part of secondary storage when RAM becomes full.
Magnetic storage such as a hard disk drive uses spinning platters and read/write heads.
Optical storage such as a CD or DVD is read using a laser.
Solid state storage uses flash memory with no moving parts, making it fast and durable.

cards.title: Networks, Topologies and Protocols
lines: A LAN connects devices over a small area such as one building.
A WAN connects devices over a large geographical area, such as the internet.
In a client-server network, a central server manages resources for client devices.
In a peer-to-peer network, devices share resources directly with no central server.
In a star topology, every device connects to a central switch or hub.
TCP/IP controls how data is packaged and addressed for transfer across networks.
HTTPS uses encryption to send web data securely.

cards.title: Searching and Sorting Algorithms
lines: A linear search checks each item in turn until the target is found.
A binary search repeatedly halves a sorted list to find the target quickly.
A binary search only works if the list is already sorted.
A bubble sort compares neighbouring items and swaps them until the list is ordered.
A merge sort splits the list in half repeatedly, then merges the parts in order.
An insertion sort builds a sorted list by inserting each item into its correct place.

cards.title: Forms of Attack and Security Measures
lines: Phishing tricks users into revealing details through fake emails or websites.
A brute force attack tries many passwords until the correct one is found.
A DDoS attack floods a server with traffic so it cannot respond to real users.
Malware includes viruses, worms, Trojan horses, spyware, and ransomware.
A firewall monitors traffic and blocks unauthorised connections.
Two-step verification adds a second check beyond a username and password.

cards.title: Logic Gates and Boolean Operators
lines: An AND gate outputs 1 only when both inputs are 1.
An OR gate outputs 1 when at least one input is 1.
A NOT gate inverts the input, turning 1 into 0 and 0 into 1.
A NAND gate is an AND gate followed by a NOT, so it outputs 0 only when both inputs are 1.
A NOR gate outputs 1 only when both inputs are 0.
An XOR gate outputs 1 when the two inputs are different.
A truth table lists the output for every possible combination of inputs.

cards.title: Software, Operating Systems and Translators
lines: System software runs the computer, while application software helps the user do tasks.
The operating system manages files, memory, peripherals, and user accounts.
An interrupt is a signal that tells the CPU a task needs immediate attention.
A high-level language is close to human language and easier to read and write.
A low-level language is close to machine code and gives fine control of hardware.
A compiler translates the whole program at once, while an interpreter translates line by line.
